narrative: As a Black male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20—right around the typical 3.21 average for students in this dataset—your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on past students' profiles to predict their chances of passing the bar exam on the first try. The model assessed your full-time enrollment plans, middle family income bracket, LSAT score of 27, and your Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings of 1st (well below the averages of about 5.7 and 5.5, respectively), among other details. It calculated a 90.8% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which is the threshold that led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting those predicted to pass and succeed in their legal careers. The three biggest factors pushing this prediction toward failure were your Year 3 academic decile, your Year 1 academic decile, and your LSAT score, in that order of influence. Your low Year 3 decile ranking had the strongest pull toward the failure prediction, signaling to the model that your later undergraduate performance placed you in the bottom tier compared to peers who typically pass the bar. Similarly, your Year 1 decile being at the very bottom amplified this concern, as early struggles often correlate with bar exam challenges in the model's view of historical data. Even your LSAT score, though not the top driver, further nudged the prediction toward failure by falling notably short of the 36.7 average seen in successful bar takers. These elements outweighed positives like your near-average GPA and full-time commitment, which is common for 93% of students and didn't sway the outcome much here. Compared to typical students who pass, your profile shows weaker early and mid-stage academic rankings, putting you outside the stronger performers the model favors. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man pursuing law amid real barriers—many overcome similar starts—but a data-driven call based on patterns from thousands of past cases. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the average, strengthening your high school or undergrad deciles through additional coursework or post-bac programs, and gathering strong recommendation letters highlighting your resilience. Reapplying after addressing these could shift your predicted odds significantly, opening doors at schools that value your unique perspective in the legal field. Keep pushing; your story is far from over.
